stages: Explore what movement skills look like in different environments through demonstration and observation
Identify key differences between familiar and unfamiliar movement situations
Define what successful transfer of skills means across environments
Establish criteria for adapting and creating new movement patterns
student_prompts: What movement skills do you use in different places and how do they change?
When have you had to adjust your movements for a new environment or situation?
What makes it easier or harder to use your movement skills in different places?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select movement examples that clearly show progression from basic skills in one environment to successful transfer across multiple environments.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to demonstrate movement skills.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can demonstrate basic movement skills with help in familiar places.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can apply movement skills on my own in familiar environments but find it challenging to use them in new situations.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can develop, apply and transfer movement skills effectively across different environments and situations independently.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can adapt and create movement skills fluently across complex and unfamiliar environments.

self_check_prompts: Can I use my movement skills successfully in different environments without help?
How well do I adapt my movements when the situation changes?

no_evidence: Provide guided practice with basic movement skills in a safe, familiar environment
Use demonstration and physical prompts to support initial movement attempts
emerging: Gradually reduce support while practicing familiar movements
Introduce slight variations in the same environment to build confidence
developing: Practice the same movement skills in different environments with guidance
Help identify similarities between familiar and new movement situations
competent: Challenge with complex or unfamiliar environments requiring skill adaptation
Encourage experimentation with creating new movement patterns

stages: Brainstorm what students already know about healthy eating and nutrition.
Discuss what makes a diet 'balanced' and identify key nutrition concepts.
Explore connections between food choices and how our bodies feel and function.
Define different levels of understanding from basic identification to complex evaluation.
student_prompts: What do you think makes a meal or diet healthy?
How do the foods we eat affect how we feel and perform?
What's the difference between knowing nutrition facts and understanding how they work together?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that show clear progression from listing food groups to explaining complex diet-health relationships.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to explain balanced diet principles.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some nutrition concepts with help but my explanations need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain basic balanced diet principles but struggle to connect nutrition with health impacts.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain balanced diet principles and clearly describe how nutrition impacts physical health and well-being.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can explain nutrition principles and evaluate complex relationships between diet choices and health outcomes.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain what makes a diet balanced without just listing food groups?
Can I connect specific nutrition choices to their effects on health and well-being?

no_evidence: Provide graphic organizers to help organize thoughts about nutrition.
Start with familiar foods and guide identification of their nutritional components.
emerging: Use questioning to help clarify and expand incomplete explanations.
Provide sentence starters to structure more complete nutrition explanations.
developing: Guide students to make explicit connections between specific nutrients and health outcomes.
Use cause-and-effect graphic organizers to link diet principles with health impacts.
competent: Challenge students to analyze real-world diet scenarios and their health implications.
Encourage evaluation of conflicting nutrition information or diet trends.

stages: Brainstorm what makes people healthy or unhealthy from student experiences
Sort brainstormed items into categories of behaviors, conditions, and situations
Discuss what it means to describe how these affect our bodies and minds
Create quality levels from basic naming to detailed explaining
student_prompts: What are some things that can make people sick or hurt their health?
How would you explain to a younger student why this affects health?
What's the difference between just naming a health risk and really understanding it?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from simple naming to detailed explanations of health impacts.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to identify any health risks

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some health risks with help but my descriptions need work

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify clear health risks on my own but struggle to explain how they affect well-being

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can recognize and describe various behaviors, conditions and situations that affect physical health and well-being

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can recognize subtle health risks and describe complex connections between multiple health factors

self_check_prompts: Can I explain how this health risk actually affects the body or mind?
Am I identifying different types of health risks including behaviors, conditions, and situations?

no_evidence: Provide examples of common health risks from student's daily life
Ask what happens when people don't get enough sleep or exercise
emerging: Guide student to explain one health risk they named in their own words
Ask follow-up questions about how the risk affects the body
developing: Prompt student to describe what happens inside the body when exposed to the risk
Encourage student to think about both immediate and long-term effects
competent: Challenge student to identify less obvious health risks in their environment
Ask student to explain connections between different health factors

stages: Introduce the concept of paying attention to what happens inside our minds and bodies
Explore what it means to notice our thoughts, feelings, and what we sense
Discuss how we can use this noticing to help ourselves learn and grow
Create language together for different levels of this skill
Refine our shared understanding through examples and discussion
student_prompts: What does it feel like when you really pay attention to your thoughts or feelings?
How might noticing what's happening inside you help you in different situations?
What would it look like if someone was just starting to learn this skill versus someone who was really good at it?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Look for student work or scenarios that clearly show different levels of internal awareness and application of that awareness to real situations.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to pay attention to my thoughts, feelings, or what I notice around me.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can sometimes notice my thoughts, feelings, or perceptions when someone helps me.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can notice my thoughts, feelings, and perceptions on my own but don't always use this awareness to help myself.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can focus on my thoughts, feelings, and perceptions and use this awareness to help myself learn and grow.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can use my self-awareness skills in new situations and connect them with deeper thinking about myself.

self_check_prompts: Am I noticing what's happening in my mind and body right now?
How am I using what I notice about myself to help me in this situation?

no_evidence: Guide student to pause and notice one simple internal experience
Provide sentence stems like 'Right now I notice...' or 'I'm feeling...'
emerging: Encourage independent noticing without prompts
Help student identify patterns in their internal experiences
developing: Support student in connecting their awareness to specific actions or choices
Ask how their self-awareness could help them in this moment
competent: Challenge student to apply their self-awareness in unfamiliar contexts
Encourage deeper reflection on how self-awareness connects to their growth

stages: Students share what it means to 'look back' at experiences and why this might be valuable.
Class discusses what makes some experiences more meaningful or memorable than others.
Students explore what patterns, impacts, and lessons might look like through guided questioning.
Groups create criteria for what strong reflection and analysis would include.
Class finalizes rubric levels by comparing and refining group contributions.
student_prompts: What does it mean to really think deeply about something that happened to you?
How can looking at multiple experiences help you notice things you might have missed?
What's the difference between just remembering what happened and actually learning from it?
How would you know if someone has done a really good job analyzing their experiences?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student reflection samples that clearly show different depths of analysis, from simple retelling to sophisticated pattern recognition across multiple experiences.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to analyze my past experiences.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify basic details from my experiences with help but struggle to see patterns.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify some patterns or impacts from my experiences but my analysis needs more depth.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can analyze my past experiences to identify clear patterns, impacts, and meaningful lessons learned.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can transfer insights across diverse experiences to generate sophisticated understanding and predictions beyond the immediate situation.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain not just what happened but what it means and how it connects to other experiences?
Have I identified specific patterns, impacts, or lessons that I could apply to future situations?

no_evidence: Provide a simple reflection prompt about one specific recent experience.
Model thinking aloud about your own experience to demonstrate the process.
emerging: Ask guided questions to help identify one clear pattern or connection between experiences.
Provide sentence starters for deeper analysis.
developing: Challenge to explain the 'so what' of identified patterns or impacts.
Encourage connections between current analysis and potential future applications.
competent: Prompt to apply analytical approach to experiences from different contexts or time periods.
Encourage prediction-making based on identified patterns.

stages: Students share examples of times they learned from past experiences.
Class discusses what makes a good prediction about future situations.
Students identify key elements that help connect past learning to future preparation.
Groups create criteria for different levels of prediction and preparation skills.
student_prompts: What past experience taught you something useful for the future?
How do you know when a prediction is realistic and helpful?
What's the difference between a basic connection and a detailed preparation plan?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose student work that shows clear progression from simple past-future connections to detailed preparation plans with multiple scenarios.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet connected my past experiences to preparing for future situations.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some past experiences with help but make few connections to future situations.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can connect some past experiences to future situations but my predictions need more detail.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can use insights from past experiences to make realistic predictions and prepare appropriately for future challenges and opportunities.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can transfer predictive insights across different contexts and develop comprehensive backup plans for multiple scenarios.

self_check_prompts: Can I clearly explain how my past experience connects to this future situation?
Are my predictions realistic and my preparations detailed enough?

no_evidence: Help student identify one specific past experience they can reflect on.
Guide student to notice one simple connection between past and future.
emerging: Encourage student to make connections independently without prompting.
Ask student to explain why their past experience relates to the future situation.
developing: Push student to add specific details to their predictions.
Challenge student to consider what could go wrong and how to prepare.
competent: Encourage student to apply their prediction skills to different types of situations.
Ask student to develop backup plans for when their main prediction doesn't work out.

stages: Students share recent experiences that made them feel strong emotions
Class identifies different emotions and discusses what caused them
Students explore why the same experience might affect different people differently
Groups create criteria for understanding emotional impact at different levels
student_prompts: What happened that made you feel that way?
Why do you think this experience affected you like this?
How might someone else feel about the same experience?
What does it mean to really understand how experiences affect emotions?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from simple emotion identification to complex analysis of why experiences create different impacts on different people.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet shown understanding of how experiences affect emotions.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name basic emotions with help but struggle to connect them to what happened.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can recognize how experiences affect emotions in situations I know well.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how and why specific experiences create emotional impacts on myself and others.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyze complex emotional patterns and predict how different experiences will affect different people.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain why an experience made me or someone else feel a certain way?
Do I understand how the same experience might affect different people differently?

no_evidence: Guide student to notice and name emotions they are feeling
Help student connect a simple experience to how it made them feel
emerging: Ask student to explain what specifically caused the emotion they identified
Provide sentence starters to help connect experiences with emotional impacts
developing: Challenge student to consider why the experience had that emotional impact
Prompt student to think about how others might feel differently about the same experience
competent: Encourage student to analyze more complex emotional situations with multiple factors
Ask student to predict emotional impacts before experiences happen

stages: Students share examples of decisions they've made and their effects
Class discusses how decisions impact different people at different times
Students identify situations where group decisions work better than individual ones
Together create criteria for explaining decision impacts and choosing decision-making processes
student_prompts: What decisions have you made that affected other people?
How might the effects of a decision change over weeks or months?
When should a group make a decision together instead of one person deciding?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose decisions with clear short-term and long-term effects on multiple people, including both individual and group decision scenarios.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to explain how decisions affect people or when groups should decide together.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some effects of decisions with help but my explanations are incomplete.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain some decision impacts on myself and others but miss timing or group decision situations.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how decisions affect myself and others over time and identify when collective decision-making works best.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyze complex decision impacts across multiple timeframes and evaluate the best decision-making approaches.

self_check_prompts: Did I explain effects on both myself and others over different time periods?
Did I identify situations where group decisions are better than individual ones?

no_evidence: Provide a simple decision scenario and ask what might happen next
Guide student to identify one person affected by a familiar decision
emerging: Ask student to extend their thinking about who else might be affected
Prompt student to consider what happens after the immediate effect
developing: Guide student to think about effects weeks or months later
Ask when it would be better for a group to make this decision together
competent: Challenge student to consider multiple interconnected effects across different timeframes
Ask student to compare and evaluate different decision-making approaches

stages: Students brainstorm what makes something dangerous or risky in familiar situations.
Class discusses how to tell if something bad is likely to happen versus unlikely.
Students explore how to measure how serious the consequences would be if risks occurred.
Groups practice applying both likelihood and severity thinking to sample scenarios.
Class refines criteria for thorough risk assessment including complex situations.
student_prompts: What could go wrong in this situation and how would you spot the warning signs?
How do you decide if something is very likely to happen or probably won't happen?
How do you figure out if the consequences would be minor, serious, or really bad?
What makes someone good at spotting risks that others might miss?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios with varying complexity levels and clear distinctions between obvious versus hidden hazards, and between high-likelihood/low-impact versus low-likelihood/high-impact risks.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to identify hazards or assess risks.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can spot obvious dangers with help but struggle to judge how likely they are or how bad they could be.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify common hazards on my own and make basic judgments about risk with some gaps.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently spot potential hazards and accurately judge both how likely they are and how serious they could be.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can systematically identify complex hazards and evaluate risks using multiple criteria across different situations.

self_check_prompts: Have I considered both how likely this risk is and how serious the consequences would be?
Am I looking beyond the obvious hazards to spot less visible dangers?

no_evidence: Guide student to notice one obvious hazard in a simple scenario.
Ask what could go wrong in a familiar situation they know well.
emerging: Prompt student to consider how often this type of problem actually happens.
Help student think about whether the consequences would be minor or major.
developing: Challenge student to look for less obvious hazards they might have missed.
Ask student to explain their reasoning for both likelihood and severity judgments.
competent: Introduce scenarios with multiple interconnected risks or hidden hazards.
Encourage student to consider how risks might change in different contexts or conditions.

stages: Students share examples of different groups they belong to and rules within those groups.
Students identify what makes group rules different from individual choices.
Students explore how group rules affect how people act and feel.
Students distinguish between simple rule-following and complex group interactions.
student_prompts: What groups are you part of and what rules do they have?
How do group rules change the way people behave?
What happens when someone follows or breaks group expectations?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work that shows clear differences in depth of explanation and connection between group dynamics and individual effects.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet explained how group rules or norms work.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some group rules with help but my explanations need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain basic group rules and norms but struggle to show how they affect individuals.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how rules, norms, and behaviors work in different groups and how they affect individuals.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyze complex interactions between multiple group norms and predict how individuals will respond.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain both the group rules and how they affect people?
Have I shown understanding of different types of groups and situations?

no_evidence: Provide examples of familiar groups and ask students to identify one rule.
Use graphic organizers to help students see group structure.
emerging: Ask students to explain why a group rule exists.
Guide students to give specific examples rather than general statements.
developing: Prompt students to describe how a group rule makes someone feel or act differently.
Ask students to compare the same person's behavior in two different groups.
competent: Challenge students to examine what happens when group norms conflict.
Ask students to predict how changing one norm might affect individual behavior.

stages: Students share examples of different groups they belong to and brainstorm what makes each group unique.
Students identify specific rules or expectations that exist in each group they mentioned.
Students discuss what happens when someone follows or doesn't follow these group expectations.
Students create a progression from needing help with social rules to helping others navigate them.
student_prompts: What are some different groups or situations you're part of at school, home, or in your community?
What are the unwritten rules or expectations in each of these places?
How do you know when you're following the social norms well in different situations?
What does it look like when someone is really skilled at adapting to different social situations?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear differences in social expectations across familiar contexts like classroom discussions versus lunch time or family dinner versus sports practice.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet shown that I can recognize or follow social norms in different situations.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some social rules when someone helps me but I struggle to follow them consistently.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can recognize social norms in familiar situations but don't always apply appropriate behavior on my own.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently recognize and follow the rules and norms of different groups and situations consistently.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can adapt my behavior fluidly across diverse social contexts and help others navigate social norms.

self_check_prompts: Can I identify what behavior is expected in this situation without being told?
Do I consistently follow the social norms even when no one is watching or reminding me?

no_evidence: Point out specific social expectations in the current situation.
Model appropriate behavior and explain why it fits the context.
emerging: Ask the student to identify one social norm they notice in the current situation.
Provide gentle reminders before transitions to new social contexts.
developing: Encourage the student to pause and consider what behavior is expected before acting.
Have the student reflect on how their behavior matched or didn't match the social context.
competent: Challenge the student to notice and adapt to subtle differences between similar social situations.
Ask the student to help a peer who is struggling with social norms.

stages: Students share personal values and discuss where these might come from.
Students examine how family, friends, media, and community shape what people believe is important.
Students compare their values with classmates to identify patterns of similarity and difference.
Students practice evaluating connections between social influences and values using real scenarios.
student_prompts: What are three things you value most and where do you think these values came from?
How might someone from a different background have different values than you?
What social influences have the strongest impact on shaping people's values?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear connections between specific social influences and resulting values, representing different cultural backgrounds and age-appropriate contexts.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to evaluate values or social influences.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some values with help but struggle to connect them to social influences.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify values and social influences but my evaluation of connections has gaps or mistakes.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can evaluate how values are shaped by social influences and recognise similarities and differences between people's values accurately.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can transfer my evaluation skills to analyze complex value systems across diverse cultural or historical contexts.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain how at least two social influences have shaped specific values?
Have I identified both similarities and differences between different people's values?

no_evidence: Provide a simple values identification activity with clear examples.
Guide student to name one personal value and brainstorm where it might come from.
emerging: Help student make explicit connections between identified values and their sources.
Provide sentence starters to link values to social influences.
developing: Point out specific gaps in reasoning and ask student to reconsider those connections.
Encourage student to check their evaluation against evidence or examples.
competent: Challenge student to apply their evaluation skills to unfamiliar cultural contexts.
Invite student to analyze more complex or nuanced value systems.

stages: Students brainstorm different types of relationships they know from their lives and media.
Class sorts relationship examples into categories and discusses what makes each type unique.
Students examine how one relationship type changes over time using personal or fictional examples.
Groups identify factors that make relationships more or less complex.
Class creates shared language for describing relationship dynamics and change patterns.
student_prompts: What different types of relationships do you see in your daily life?
How has one of your relationships changed since you first met that person?
What makes some relationships more complicated than others?
What signs tell you a relationship is getting stronger or weaker?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that clearly show different relationship types and include obvious changes over time or varying levels of complexity.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to identify relationship types or explain how they work.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some relationship types with help but my explanations need more work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify basic relationship types on my own but need help explaining how they change and become complex.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can identify different types of relationships and explain how relationships change over time and vary in complexity.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyze relationship patterns across different situations and predict how specific factors influence relationship development.

self_check_prompts: Can I name at least three different types of relationships and explain what makes each one unique?
Can I describe how relationships change over time and what makes some relationships more complex than others?

no_evidence: Provide sentence starters for naming relationship types from student's own experience.
Use visual organizers to help student categorize different relationships they observe.
emerging: Ask student to elaborate on their relationship examples with specific details.
Guide student to compare two relationship types and identify key differences.
developing: Prompt student to trace how one specific relationship has evolved over time.
Ask student to identify what factors make certain relationships more challenging or complex.
competent: Challenge student to find patterns in how relationships develop across different contexts.
Ask student to predict what might happen to a relationship given specific changes or circumstances.

stages: Students share examples of conflicts they've experienced or witnessed
Class discusses what makes conflicts easy or hard to resolve
Students brainstorm different strategies people use to handle disagreements
Groups sort strategies from least to most effective and discuss when to ask for help
student_prompts: What does a conflict look like and sound like?
What strategies have you seen work well when people disagree?
When might you need to ask an adult or friend for help with a conflict?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear differences between basic strategies and more sophisticated approaches, including situations where seeking support was necessary.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet shown that I can identify conflicts or use strategies to resolve them.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can recognize some conflicts with help but my strategies don't always work well.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify conflicts on my own and use basic strategies but find complex disagreements challenging.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can identify conflicts and choose appropriate strategies to resolve them while asking for support when I need it.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can use conflict resolution skills in new situations and help others work through their disagreements.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain what the conflict is really about?
Did I choose a strategy that fits this situation and ask for help if needed?

no_evidence: Point out specific moments when conflicts occur in classroom situations
Model naming the problem and one simple strategy to try
emerging: Practice identifying the main issue before jumping to solutions
Discuss which strategies work best for different types of conflicts
developing: Analyze what makes some conflicts more complex than others
Practice recognizing when a situation requires adult support or mediation
competent: Explore how the same strategies apply in different settings or relationships
Reflect on how to guide others through conflict resolution steps

stages: Students brainstorm what makes them feel safe with friends and family.
Class discusses warning signs that might indicate relationship problems.
Students explore different ways to respond when they notice safety concerns.
Groups create scenarios and practice identifying safety characteristics.
Class finalizes rubric levels based on their collective understanding.
student_prompts: What does it feel like when you're in a safe relationship with someone?
How might you know if a relationship isn't safe anymore?
What are some good ways to respond when you notice safety problems?
How could you help a friend recognize relationship safety issues?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that show clear safety indicators and realistic response options that students can relate to from their own social experiences.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet shown that I can identify what makes relationships safe.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some basic safety features with help but my responses need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify obvious safety characteristics but struggle to respond well when safety is threatened.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can identify characteristics of safe relationships and respond appropriately when safety is threatened.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can recognize safety issues in complex situations and help others maintain relationship safety.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain what makes a relationship feel safe without needing help?
Do I know what to do when I notice relationship safety problems?

no_evidence: Guide student to notice one specific safety characteristic in a simple relationship scenario.
Provide sentence starters for describing what safety feels like.
emerging: Practice identifying safety features without prompts using familiar examples.
Role-play one appropriate response to a clear safety concern.
developing: Discuss multiple response options when safety is threatened and practice choosing the best one.
Analyze scenarios where safety threats are less obvious.
competent: Explore complex relationship dynamics where safety issues are subtle or layered.
Practice coaching others through safety recognition and response strategies.
